0: <title>Carbon Footprint Analysis of Bioenergy Production from Cattle Manure in the Brazilian Central-West </title>
Ana M. N. Herrera 1 . Elisa M. M. Esteves 1 Cláudia R. V. Morgado 1 Victor P. P. Esteves 1
Received: 25 March 2020 / Accepted: 30 October 2020 / Published online: 6 November 2020 Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2020
1: This article reports a carbon footprint analysis of bioelectricity generation from cattle manure obtained from feedlot systems in the Brazilian Central-West region. The analyses were performed by applying two different energy source scenarios: Scl-using external energy in an anaerobic digestion (AD) plant; and Sc2-recirculating part of the energy generated in a combined heat and power (CHP) plant, with two approaches (allocation and system expansion) for sharing impacts between the products and co- products obtained in the cycle. In addition, the generation of electricity from manure was compared with generation from natural gas (reference system). The greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions were calculated using data from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change Guidelines and the SimaPro software. Sc2 was the best scenario, since the energy recirculation from the system reduces emissions from the AD plant by up to 83.61%. Both scenarios with system expansion present emissions ~4.5 times greater than when allocation was applied, because allocation splits emissions between products obtained, removing the produc- tion chain's responsibility for the co-products generated. The manure management was responsible for more than 65% of emissions in both scenarios, making emissions in both scenarios higher than the reference system. There is a need to improve distribution logistics of AD-CHP plant products in the Central-West region, since bioenergy generation can mitigate GHG emissions from livestock activity by turning manure residue cost into a gain, and generating products with energy and market value, to make the process more efficient and environmentally friendly.
Keywords Cattle manure biogas . Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions . Carbon footprint analysis . Anaerobic digestion (AD) Combined heat and power (CHP)

4: <header>Highlights </header>

<list>Carbon footprint analysis of bioenergy from cattle manure was performed. 
Recycling of heat and electricity as inputs of the AD plant makes the system environmentally friendlier. 
Generating bioenergy from manure is an important way to mitigate GHG emissions from livestock. 
The use of manure for energy generation should be encouraged due to its high energy potential.</list>

in many countries, due to the depletion of fossil resources and the pollution they produce. Among renewable energy sources, biomass represents 73% of the total renewable primary energy supply in the world [1].
1 Environmental Engineering Program (PEA), Federal University of Rio de Janeiro, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil

Proven global reserves of natural gas fell slightly in 2018, when they were judged sufficient to meet 50.9 years at current consumption levels [2]. This indicates an urgent need to ob- tain new gas sources to meet future demand, since gas is widely used, mainly to generate heat and electricity and for cooking. Biogas is an alternative to replace natural gas [3].

The interest in biogas production, from anaerobic digestion (AD) technology, has grown rapidly since the 1970s [4]. This growth has occurred mainly due to (a) the increasing impor- tance of renewable energy to reduce greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions [5, 6]; (b) the need for sustainable management of organic waste [7, 8], with reduction of its disposal in landfills [9]; and (c) the rising energy prices with fossil fuel depletion.

Biogas plants can be fed with numerous raw materials, including agropastoral, industrial, and domestic byproducts and wastes. Cattle manure is one of the possible feedstocks of AD plants, with the advantage that besides producing
biogas, it also generates digestate, a biofertilizer with higher nutrient content than fresh manure [8, 10].

Manure is generated by livestock breeding, which is re- sponsible for 18% of world emissions of carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2 eq.) [11, 12], especially due to enteric fer- mentation and manure management [13].

Global beef consumption has increased in recent decades, and Brazil has become the world's largest producer and ex- porter [14]. Of the country's five official regions, the Central- West has the largest cattle herd (Fig. 1), with for more than 75 million head, 34% of the national total (218 million). Cattle herd increased 47% between 1981 and 2016.

Despite of the herd expansion shown in Fig. 1, grazing areas in the Central-West region decreased, indicating that this region is among the top priority areas in Brazil to adopt inten- sified livestock breeding [16]. This is an important trend, be- cause the large amount of organic waste generated can be sustainably managed for use as feedstock to produce biogas when animals are kept in feedlots, since it is uneconomic to collect the manure from pastures.

Biogas generated in AD plants can deliver various types of energy, such as electricity, heat, steam, and combined heat and power (CHP). In particular, the gas can be supplied to the grid or used as a transportation fuel [17, 18]. These characteristics make the production of biogas highly relevant for the political objectives of various countries that want to increase their share of renewable energy production and improve resource effi- ciency [19, 20]. In this sense, some studies have assessed the net environmental impacts of the whole bioenergy life cycle or the cycle until the biogas production. These studies used dif- ferent methods of apportioning impacts between products. Russo and von Blottnitz [21] studied the biogas production in South Africa using mass allocation between fresh meat, food/feed byproducts, manure, and inedible raw materials. Mezzullo et al. [22] used the mass and economic allocation for the two outputs from the plant (biogas and digestate). Lansche and Muller [23] considered that the electricity gener- ated from biogas replaces the marginal mixture of

conventional energy generation based on fossil fuels. Bacenetti et al. [24] and Lijo et al. [25] assessed the electricity production life cycle using a substitution method, where the digestate replaces mineral fertilizers. Cherubini et al. [26] have performed life cycle assessment of biogas production consid- ering the substitution of manure used in soil replacing chem- ical fertilizer. Some papers considered electricity as the only product, while the co-generated heat can be recycled to the plant [27] or discarded in the environment [28], and thus, they did not consider allocation in the energy production.

None of the works analyze the life cycle of bioenergy pro- duction from manure, detailing different approaches for the valorization of co-products obtained in the chain. Thus, this study was carried out considering different scenarios, to eval- uate the GHG emissions of manure bioenergy generation from Brazilian Central-West, identifying the best strategy for harnessing the products generated to add value to manure and encourage its use within the livestock chain while reduc- ing its GHG emissions.

9: This article reports a carbon footprint analysis of bioelectricity generation from cattle manure obtained from feedlot systems in the Brazilian Central-West region. In addition, the genera- tion of electricity from manure was compared with generation from natural gas (reference system), since biogas is its main substitute, to identify whether its production and use is bene- ficial to the bioenergy system. The boundaries of the study included stages of manure management (divided into pit stor- age below animal confinement area and manure storage in open tanks), manure transportation, an AD plant where biogas is produced and a combined heat and power (CHP) plant, where electricity and heat are generated.

The proposed scenarios considered anaerobic digestion followed by combined heat and power generation, consider- ing (Scl) external heat and electricity for AD and (Sc2) recirculating part of the energy from these sources (Fig. 2). The scenarios were analyzed by two approaches (allocation and system expansion) for apportioning the impact of the products and co-products obtained, to compare the different approaches regarding the use of the co-products generated. In the productive chain under study, besides biogas, it is gener- ated digestate as a co-product of anaerobic digestion. Digestate has been widely used as an organic fertilizer for crops [29] due to its high nitrogen and phosphorus content [30]. In CHP plants, besides electricity, heat is generated as a co-product. This work considered the integration of CHP and AD plants, allowing the use of energy generated from CHP to feed the AD plant.
Manure can be considered a residue or byproduct of the livestock chain, depending on its subsequent use [31]. In the
Fig. 2 Life cycle of biogas energy production for the different scenarios under analysis

(a) Manure Management Manure Transport Manure I AD Plant Biogas Digestate||(b) Manure Management Manure Transport Manure AD Plant Biogas Digestate CHP Plant Heat Electricity||CHP Plant Heat Electricity

Brazilian scenario, due to the low technology development to use manure as a substrate in biogas production, it is considered a residue of animal breeding. Thus, livestock breeding and enteric fermentation emissions are outside this study's boundaries.

GHG emission is the most important category to quantify the global environmental impact of human activities [32]. These emissions were assessed in this work because the bioenergy production chain affects not only the specific re- gion, it has global environmental impact. The calculation is focused on GHG emissions per area in a representative cattle farming region in Brazilian Central-West region: Dourados micro-region (DMR), which is the third largest beef producing state in Brazil [33]. This micro-region has warm temperature (annual mean of 28 °C) [34] and includes 15 municipalities (Amambai, Antônio João, Aral Moreira, Caarapó, Douradina, Dourados, Fátima do Sul, Itaporã, Juti, Laguna Carapã, Maracaju, Nova Alvorada do Sul, Ponta Porã, Rio Brilhante Vicentina). Based on the regional approach, the functional unit was defined as hectare, to obtain the emissions in kg CO2 eq. per ha. All the GHG emissions obtained from the
DMR were divided by the total area (3,735,900 ha), allowing comparative analysis of regions with different areas and inten- sive livestock production.

The emissions from manure management were estimated in accordance with the ISO 14044 method [35], using data from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas Inventories-Tier 2 [36]. The GHG emissions from transportation, AD plant, and CHP plant steps were obtained from the Ecoinvent 3.0 data- base, using the SimaPro 8.0 LCA software, with the single impact category Climate Change from the IPCC 2013 GWP environmental assessment method and 100-year time horizon.

The boundaries of the study included stages of manure management (divided into pit stor- age below animal confinement area and manure storage in open tanks), manure transportation, an AD plant where biogas is produced and a combined heat and power (CHP) plant, where electricity and heat are generated.

The proposed scenarios considered anaerobic digestion followed by combined heat and power generation, consider- ing (Scl) external heat and electricity for AD and (Sc2) recirculating part of the energy from these sources (Fig. 2). The scenarios were analyzed by two approaches (allocation and system expansion) for apportioning the impact of the products and co-products obtained, to compare the different approaches regarding the use of the co-products generated. In the productive chain under study, besides biogas, it is gener- ated digestate as a co-product of anaerobic digestion. Digestate has been widely used as an organic fertilizer for crops [29] due to its high nitrogen and phosphorus content [30]. In CHP plants, besides electricity, heat is generated as a co-product. This work considered the integration of CHP and AD plants, allowing the use of energy generated from CHP to feed the AD plant.
Manure can be considered a residue or byproduct of the livestock chain, depending on its subsequent use [31]. In the

(a) Manure Management Manure Transport Manure I AD Plant Biogas Digestate||(b) Manure Management Manure Transport Manure AD Plant Biogas Digestate CHP Plant Heat Electricity||CHP Plant Heat Electricity

Brazilian scenario, due to the low technology development to use manure as a substrate in biogas production, it is considered a residue of animal breeding. Thus, livestock breeding and enteric fermentation emissions are outside this study's boundaries.

GHG emission is the most important category to quantify the global environmental impact of human activities [32]. These emissions were assessed in this work because the bioenergy production chain affects not only the specific re- gion, it has global environmental impact. The calculation is focused on GHG emissions per area in a representative cattle farming region in Brazilian Central-West region: Dourados micro-region (DMR), which is the third largest beef producing state in Brazil [33]. This micro-region has warm temperature (annual mean of 28 °C) [34] and includes 15 municipalities (Amambai, Antônio João, Aral Moreira, Caarapó, Douradina, Dourados, Fátima do Sul, Itaporã, Juti, Laguna Carapã, Maracaju, Nova Alvorada do Sul, Ponta Porã, Rio Brilhante Vicentina). Based on the regional approach, the functional unit was defined as hectare, to obtain the emissions in kg CO2 eq. per ha. All the GHG emissions obtained from the
DMR were divided by the total area (3,735,900 ha), allowing comparative analysis of regions with different areas and inten- sive livestock production.

The emissions from manure management were estimated in accordance with the ISO 14044 method [35], using data from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas Inventories-Tier 2 [36]. The GHG emissions from transportation, AD plant, and CHP plant steps were obtained from the Ecoinvent 3.0 data- base, using the SimaPro 8.0 LCA software, with the single impact category Climate Change from the IPCC 2013 GWP environmental assessment method and 100-year time horizon.

13: <header>Method to Calculate Manure Generation in the Micro- Region </header>
The amount of manure produced by an animal during the confinement period is given by Eq. (1).

<list>M is the total manure produced from each animal, in kg [animal] day 
mf is the final mass of each animal, in kg. The final mass for the region is 350 kg for young cattle and 500 kg for adult cattle; 
mg is the daily mass gain, in kg day It was used 1.3 kg [37]; 
n represents each day of confinement, ranging from 0 to nt, expressed in days (d); 
nt is the total days in confinement. The time under confinement considered for each animal is 90 days [37, 38]; and 
R is the manure rate from the animal mass. The value adopted is 0.08 [39].</list>

The total of manure generated per animal during confine- ment is obtained by the sum of the manure generated per day (from Eq. 1). Considering that each animal remains 90 days in confinement, a total of 295,202 head of cattle were slaughtered during 2014 in the Dourados micro-region (Supplementary Material). Here, only 10.44% of this total was considered, which is the proportion of confined cattle in Brazil [40]. Table 1 presents the total manure generated in the DMR by confined animals.

15: <header>Method to calculate greenhouse gas emissions due to manure management </header>

According to the IPCC Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas Inventories-Tier 2 [36], manure management results in methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) emissions, with the relative portions varying significantly among types of man- agement systems used. This method was used to determine emissions from two types of manure management: pit storage below animal confinement areas and storage in open tanks. The methane emission of a manure management category (EFCH4), expressed in kilograms of CO2 per animal per year, was obtained by applying Eq. 2.

<list>EFCH4 is the methane emission from manure management, expressed in kg CO2 eq. [animal] ¹ 
B is the maximum methane production capacity for manure, expressed in [m³ of CH4] [kg of VS excreted] obtained from IPCC [36] for Latin America, Table 10A-5). The value used was 0.1 m³ of CH4 [kg of VS] 
0.67 is the conversion factor from m³ of CH4 to kilograms of CH4. 
MCF is the methane conversion factor for manure management system (varies according to the system and local temperature), expressed in percentage (Table 10.17, IPCC [36]). The value used was 80% for pit storage below animal and 5% for manure storage in open tanks. 
28 is the factor of global warming potential (GWP) for 100 years of CH4 according to the IPCC [41], used to convert the emissions result into kilograms of carbon dioxide equivalent. 
nt is the number of days under confinement for each animal, expressed in days y-1. A time period (nt) of 90 days was used for each animal confinement [37, 38]. 
VS is the daily volatile solid excreted, measured in kg dry matter [animal] ¹ day The daily VS excreted is 8% [21] of the cattle manure generated per day (Eq. 1).</list>
There are two N20 emissions from manure manage- ment: direct and indirect. The direct emissions (EF N20) were calculated based on Eq. 2 and the indirect ones on Eq. 3.

22: <header>Inventories of Anaerobic Digestion (AD) and Combined Heat and Power (CHP) Plants </header>

Although Mato Grosso do Sul is one of the country's leading states in terms of cattle herd size, it does not have biogas plants that utilize manure as raw material. For this study, biogas plants with the capacity to support 100% of the manure pro- duced in the micro-region in feedlot systems were considered.

AD plants were considered with covered anaerobic lagoons to stabilize organic matter in manure. The mixing is responsi- ble for increasing the degradation efficiency, and consequent- ly, the amount of biogas produced.

This system uses electricity and heat for the biogas gener- ation process. Together with the biogas, digestate is produced as a byproduct of the AD plant. Biogas can subsequently be used as fuel in a CHP plant to generate electricity and heat [42].

The inputs per hectare for AD and CHP plants were obtain- ed by the inventory of 1 m³ of biogas generated (personal communication from regional companies). The amount of ma- nure necessary to generate 1 m³ of biogas (42.08 kg was considered to estimate the total biogas generated in the DMR (Table 1). This value is similar to the data obtained in Hijazi et al. [43] (45.22 kg m 3, and in the same order of magnitude of data in Energy Inventory of Rural Waste [44] (69.4kgm-3). To obtain the regional data, the biogas obtained (2,016,309.95 m³ in DMR) was divided by the total DMR area, as presented in Table 3.

Based on a composition of 55% CH4 and 45% CO2, biogas has a lower heating value of 18,370 kJ m [27]. The total energy (electricity and heat) generated in the biogas plant under study (11,795.06 kJ corresponds to only 64.21% of biogas energy content. The plant has a rudimentary CHP system, with low efficiency of energy conversion.

AD plants in Brazil have an average output of 1373 m3 of biogas per day [45]. Since the micro-region produces 232,428 kg of manure per day, approximately four AD plants would be necessary to supply all biogas that can be generated from manure in the region (5524.14 m³ per day).

28: According to ISO 14044 [35], for multiple output processes, it is necessary to apportion emissions among the given products, using sub-process division, system expansion, or allocation. However, it is not possible to divide the production of biogas and digestate in the AD plant, or the generation of electricity and heat in the CHP plant, into two independent sub-process- es. This occurs because there is no way to evaluate which inputs and outputs pertain to each sub-process.

Thus, this study applied the allocation and system expan- sion approaches, to compare the life cycle impacts of electric- ity generation from different routes of harnessing manure bio- gas. Allocation is used to share the emissions corresponding to each product according to its production percentage. On the other hand, the system expansion involves the use of products and co-products obtained in the chain, avoiding their produc- tion in other production chains. Thus, both scenarios were analyzed considering allocation (Sc1-A and Sc2-A) and sys- tem expansion (Sc1-S and Sc2-S) apportionment approaches.
29: The market value allocation was applied to the product and co-product of the AD process due to the large consumer mar- ket involved and the real potential for utilization of the main
Fig. 3 Anaerobic digestion plants locations in the Dourados micro-region

product (biogas) and the co-product (digestate). Besides that, these products cannot be compared by energy parameters be- cause they have different purposes. Biogas is usually used to generate electricity and heat, while digestate is used as fertilizer.

Biogas density is 1.28 kg for each cubic meter (kg m-3) [27], and 4.207 kg of digestate (10% of the total solids [8]) can be produced in the AD plant for each cubic meter of biogas. Considering a mean exchange rate of R$ 3.86 per dollar (av- erage for 2019), the market value of biogas is R$ 1.033 per m3 [47]. The delivered market value of digestate in Brazil is R$ 0.300 per kg (personal communication from companies). The market value allocation for biogas is 45.01% (Table 4).

Unlike the AD plant, for the CHP plant, both outputs (elec- tricity and heat) are a form of energy, and therefore, the use of energy allocation is most adequate. In addition, the heat gen- erated in the CHP plant does not have a clearly expressed economic value, since its use depends on a client (user) being close to the generation, which makes it impossible to apply the market value allocation. Table 3 gives the ratio of energy generated from 1 m³ of biogas: 4567.41 kJ of electricity and 7227.65 kJ of heat. Table 5 presents the energy allocation used for the CHP plant.

Table 9 presents the emissions of each step (in kg CO2 eq. per hectare) calculated for the different apportionment ap- proaches considered in the two scenarios. The results for Sc1-A and Sc2-A were obtained by applying the accumulated allocation of steps before the CHP plant (17.43%) and elec- tricity allocation for the CHP step (38.72%) in the emissions obtained in Table 7.

The comparison of the scenarios reveals that manure man- agement step (pit and open tank storage) is the major contrib- utor to GHG emissions, which occurs due to the large emis- sions of methane and nitrogen from manure.

Transportation step has low contribution of emissions (up to 12.02%) in the bioenergy life cycle despite the long dis- tances traveled by trucks and their consumption of diesel. Road transport is the only viable alternative in the Center- West. The transport step could be ignored if small rudimentary AD plants were considered. Barring this arrangement, the amount of energy generated does not need transportation since it is used by the farm itself.

The GHG emissions of the scenarios under analysis, in ascending order, are Sc2-A, Sc1-A, Sc2-S, and Sc1S. The total emissions of Sc2 presented a reduction, in relation to Scl, of 5.77%, applying allocation and 8.04% applying system expansion. In Scl, clean energy is used in the AD plant since more than 80% of electricity in Brazil comes from renewable sources [49]. Despite this, AD plant emis- sions in Sc2 are lower than Sc1 (60.37% when applying allocation and 83.61% when applying system expansion), by considering the recirculation of the energy generated in the CHP plant. The difference between the scenarios would be even greater if the external electricity used was gener- ated from natural gas instead of being supplied by the existing power grid.

Both scenarios with the system expansion approach present GHG emissions 4.5 times greater than when allocation is applied. This fact is explained by the fact that allocation di- vides emissions by the products obtained, removing from the productive chain the responsibility for generating co-products. The system expansion, in turn, includes this concern with co- product emissions, finding substitutes for them in the value chain, which explains the higher GHG emissions in scenarios that use this impact-sharing approach.

The different apportionment approaches, as well as the reuse of the energy generated from CHP in the AD plant itself, influence the GHG emissions results of the LCA performed. For digestate, all scenarios are adequate, since digestate has a commercial market and real potential for use as fertilizer. The digestate is necessarily produced in the biogas chain, and Brazil has a well-established mar- ket, SO its inclusion in the life cycle is solely related to the scope of the analysis.

In relation to heat, in the region studied there are few in- dustrial units where the heat could be used, which makes heat distribution logistics difficult. However, its use is extremely important for domestic and industrial purposes, since heat recovery can improve energy efficiency. The use of the sys- tem expansion apportionment approach, for both scenarios, is more suitable for Brazilian reality, since Sc2 uses part of the energy generated to supply the demand of the digestion plant. Even in Scl, which does not consider energy recirculation, the
energy generated provides an alternative to replace regional fossil energy use.

The GHG emissions of the scenarios were compared with the emissions of "electricity generation from natural gas in conventional power plants" (reference system) considering the same amount of electricity generated by the CHP plant (2465.08 kJ per hectare), giving a total of 0.876 kg of CO2 equivalent per hectare. Only the Sc2, when considering the allocation approach, presented lower emissions than electric- ity generation from natural gas. Most of the emissions of the bioenergy life cycle come from the manure management steps (pit and tank storage): 65.62% (Sc1-A), 80.37% (Sc1-S), 69.64% (Sc2-A), and 87.40% (Sc2-S). If the manure manage- ment step was disregarded, the scenarios' GHG emissions would be up to 3.4 times lower than the GHG emissions of the reference system.

Manure management emissions are independent from the bioenergy production process emissions, since manure is a residue of livestock that has to be managed. The use of manure as a raw material in the bioenergy generation chain from biogas adds value to this waste, making its management a gain instead of a cost. Thus, the bioenergy from manure can be considered an environmentally viable and clean alternative, since it mitigates emissions from the livestock chain by using manure, in addition to avoiding CO2 emissions from the natural gas production chain, as its direct substitute.

The digestate is necessarily produced in the biogas chain, and Brazil has a well-established mar- ket, SO its inclusion in the life cycle is solely related to the scope of the analysis.

In relation to heat, in the region studied there are few in- dustrial units where the heat could be used, which makes heat distribution logistics difficult. However, its use is extremely important for domestic and industrial purposes, since heat recovery can improve energy efficiency. The use of the sys- tem expansion apportionment approach, for both scenarios, is more suitable for Brazilian reality, since Sc2 uses part of the energy generated to supply the demand of the digestion plant. Even in Scl, which does not consider energy recirculation, the
energy generated provides an alternative to replace regional fossil energy use.

The GHG emissions of the scenarios were compared with the emissions of "electricity generation from natural gas in conventional power plants" (reference system) considering the same amount of electricity generated by the CHP plant (2465.08 kJ per hectare), giving a total of 0.876 kg of CO2 equivalent per hectare. Only the Sc2, when considering the allocation approach, presented lower emissions than electric- ity generation from natural gas. Most of the emissions of the bioenergy life cycle come from the manure management steps (pit and tank storage): 65.62% (Sc1-A), 80.37% (Sc1-S), 69.64% (Sc2-A), and 87.40% (Sc2-S). If the manure manage- ment step was disregarded, the scenarios' GHG emissions would be up to 3.4 times lower than the GHG emissions of the reference system.

Manure management emissions are independent from the bioenergy production process emissions, since manure is a residue of livestock that has to be managed. The use of manure as a raw material in the bioenergy generation chain from biogas adds value to this waste, making its management a gain instead of a cost. Thus, the bioenergy from manure can be considered an environmentally viable and clean alternative, since it mitigates emissions from the livestock chain by using manure, in addition to avoiding CO2 emissions from the natural gas production chain, as its direct substitute.

In Scl, clean energy is used in the AD plant since more than 80% of electricity in Brazil comes from renewable sources [49]. Despite this, AD plant emis- sions in Sc2 are lower than Sc1 (60.37% when applying allocation and 83.61% when applying system expansion), by considering the recirculation of the energy generated in the CHP plant. Both scenarios with the system expansion approach present GHG emissions 4.5 times greater than when allocation is applied. This fact is explained by the fact that allocation di- vides emissions by the products obtained, removing from the productive chain the responsibility for generating co-products. The system expansion, in turn, includes this concern with co- product emissions, finding substitutes for them in the value chain, which explains the higher GHG emissions in scenarios that use this impact-sharing approach.

The different apportionment approaches, as well as the reuse of the energy generated from CHP in the AD plant itself, influence the GHG emissions results of the LCA performed. For digestate, all scenarios are adequate, since digestate has a commercial market and real potential for use as fertilizer. The digestate is necessarily produced in the biogas chain, and Brazil has a well-established mar- ket, SO its inclusion in the life cycle is solely related to the scope of the analysis.

In relation to heat, in the region studied there are few in- dustrial units where the heat could be used, which makes heat distribution logistics difficult. However, its use is extremely important for domestic and industrial purposes, since heat recovery can improve energy efficiency. The use of the sys- tem expansion apportionment approach, for both scenarios, is more suitable for Brazilian reality, since Sc2 uses part of the energy generated to supply the demand of the digestion plant. Even in Scl, which does not consider energy recirculation, the
energy generated provides an alternative to replace regional fossil energy use.

The GHG emissions of the scenarios were compared with the emissions of "electricity generation from natural gas in conventional power plants" (reference system) considering the same amount of electricity generated by the CHP plant (2465.08 kJ per hectare), giving a total of 0.876 kg of CO2 equivalent per hectare. Only the Sc2, when considering the allocation approach, presented lower emissions than electric- ity generation from natural gas. Most of the emissions of the bioenergy life cycle come from the manure management steps (pit and tank storage): 65.62% (Sc1-A), 80.37% (Sc1-S), 69.64% (Sc2-A), and 87.40% (Sc2-S). If the manure manage- ment step was disregarded, the scenarios' GHG emissions would be up to 3.4 times lower than the GHG emissions of the reference system.

Manure management emissions are independent from the bioenergy production process emissions, since manure is a residue of livestock that has to be managed. The use of manure as a raw material in the bioenergy generation chain from biogas adds value to this waste, making its management a gain instead of a cost. Thus, the bioenergy from manure can be considered an environmentally viable and clean alternative, since it mitigates emissions from the livestock chain by using manure, in addition to avoiding CO2 emissions from the natural gas production chain, as its direct substitute.

The use of the sys- tem expansion apportionment approach, for both scenarios, is more suitable for Brazilian reality, since Sc2 uses part of the energy generated to supply the demand of the digestion plant. Even in Scl, which does not consider energy recirculation, the
energy generated provides an alternative to replace regional fossil energy use.

The GHG emissions of the scenarios were compared with the emissions of "electricity generation from natural gas in conventional power plants" (reference system) considering the same amount of electricity generated by the CHP plant (2465.08 kJ per hectare), giving a total of 0.876 kg of CO2 equivalent per hectare. Only the Sc2, when considering the allocation approach, presented lower emissions than electric- ity generation from natural gas. Most of the emissions of the bioenergy life cycle come from the manure management steps (pit and tank storage): 65.62% (Sc1-A), 80.37% (Sc1-S), 69.64% (Sc2-A), and 87.40% (Sc2-S). If the manure manage- ment step was disregarded, the scenarios' GHG emissions would be up to 3.4 times lower than the GHG emissions of the reference system.

Manure management emissions are independent from the bioenergy production process emissions, since manure is a residue of livestock that has to be managed. The use of manure as a raw material in the bioenergy generation chain from biogas adds value to this waste, making its management a gain instead of a cost. Thus, the bioenergy from manure can be considered an environmentally viable and clean alternative, since it mitigates emissions from the livestock chain by using manure, in addition to avoiding CO2 emissions from the natural gas production chain, as its direct substitute.

The comparison of the scenarios reveals that manure man- agement step (pit and open tank storage) is the major contrib- utor to GHG emissions, which occurs due to the large emis- sions of methane and nitrogen from manure.

Transportation step has low contribution of emissions (up to 12.02%) in the bioenergy life cycle despite the long dis- tances traveled by trucks and their consumption of diesel. Road transport is the only viable alternative in the Center- West. The transport step could be ignored if small rudimentary AD plants were considered. Barring this arrangement, the amount of energy generated does not need transportation since it is used by the farm itself.

The GHG emissions of the scenarios under analysis, in ascending order, are Sc2-A, Sc1-A, Sc2-S, and Sc1S. In Scl, clean energy is used in the AD plant since more than 80% of electricity in Brazil comes from renewable sources [49]. Despite this, AD plant emis- sions in Sc2 are lower than Sc1 (60.37% when applying allocation and 83.61% when applying system expansion), by considering the recirculation of the energy generated in the CHP plant. The difference between the scenarios would be even greater if the external electricity used was gener- ated from natural gas instead of being supplied by the existing power grid.

Both scenarios with the system expansion approach present GHG emissions 4.5 times greater than when allocation is applied. This fact is explained by the fact that allocation di- vides emissions by the products obtained, removing from the productive chain the responsibility for generating co-products. The system expansion, in turn, includes this concern with co- product emissions, finding substitutes for them in the value chain, which explains the higher GHG emissions in scenarios that use this impact-sharing approach.

The different apportionment approaches, as well as the reuse of the energy generated from CHP in the AD plant itself, influence the GHG emissions results of the LCA performed. For digestate, all scenarios are adequate, since digestate has a commercial market and real potential for use as fertilizer. The digestate is necessarily produced in the biogas chain, and Brazil has a well-established mar- ket, SO its inclusion in the life cycle is solely related to the scope of the analysis.

44: This article reports a carbon footprint analysis of bioelectricity generation from cattle manure obtained from feedlot systems in the Brazilian Central-West. Sc2 is considered the best scenario since the recirculation of energy from the CHP plant reduces GHG emissions from the AD plant by 60.37% (applying allocation) and 83.61% (applying system expansion), compared to Scl, which uses external energy. Considering the different apportionment ap- proaches, the total GHG emissions of Sc2 presented reduc- tions of 5.77% (allocation) and 8.04% (system expansion). The AD-CHP integration described in this work allows the use of a large part of the heat generated, which would other- wise be wasted. It is difficult to implement heat distribution logistics due to the wide geographic dispersion of industrial units in rural areas. Without energy recycling, made possible by integration, it would be necessary to use external heat that normally comes from firewood.

Both scenarios with system expansion present GHG emis- sions ~4.5 times greater than when allocation is applied. This fact is explained by the fact that allocation, unlike system expansion, distributes emissions by the products obtained, taking from the production chain responsibility for the gener- ation of co-products. Thus, the allocation approach can under- estimate or overestimate environmental impacts related to the products involved in the chain. Also, ISO recommends that allocation be avoided. In turn, system expansion should be carefully evaluated, since incorrect replacement of the prod- ucts by equivalents due to lack of details about a product can lead to an inadequate substitute choice. Manure management was responsible for more than 65% of GHG emissions in all the scenarios, resulting in higher GHG emissions than the reference system of elec- tricity generation from natural gas, in both scenarios when applying system expansion and in Sc1 when applying the allocation approach. Even so, biogas use for energy gen- eration can be considered an environmentally viable and clean alternative, since in addition to taking advantage of manure, it avoids CO2 emissions from the natural gas generation. The production of electricity and heat as well as digestate in AD-CHP plants can mitigate GHG emissions from livestock activity when considering the use of ma- nure to generate products with market value, avoiding the production of equivalent products via other systems. In addition, since livestock confinement manure needs to be managed, the use of manure as input for energy pro- duction turns this cost into a gain.
